summary: The 20 surveyed memory entries cluster into three dominant behavioral themes: scope/authorization discipline (the user's most emotionally-charged correction category — unauthorized commits, out-of-scope edits, and unsolicited infrastructure proposals), proactive risk flagging (three separate projects each rewarded or demanded the agent surface cache blast-radius, client env-var exposure, and factual corrections unprompted), and pre-write convention scanning (multiple sessions corrected the agent for writing utilities, auth guards, or env reads without first grepping for existing wrappers). Two entries — feedback_data_integrity.md and feedback_pipeline_ux.md — are 19 days old and encode rules specific to a one-off CSV/Excel data-transformation project that has no apparent successor workload. The script-design pattern (permanent placement, dry-run mode, npm isolation) and the proactive-flagging meta-behavior are both sufficiently recurrent and cross-session to warrant graduation into dedicated rules files.

insights.name: scope-and-authorization-discipline
insights.instruction: Before any file write, git commit, or push: verify the target path is inside the declared session scope, and confirm fresh in-session approval exists for the operation. Prior-turn approval is not blanket. Before proposing CI, webhooks, or pipeline infrastructure, confirm the domain already has such infrastructure — if it does not, file a proposal instead of surfacing it in the current task.

insights.name: proactive-risk-surface
insights.instruction: When reviewing caching designs, flag whether invalidation keys are scoped per-user or shared globally before writing code. When reviewing env var lists, classify each NEXT_PUBLIC_/VITE_ var as 'client-bundle required' or 'server-only' and flag the latter. When the user states something factually incorrect, surface the correct fact in one line before proceeding. These are three instances of the same meta-rule: surface blast-radius, exposure, and factual risks before the user catches them.

insights.rationale: feedback_script_design.md encodes three distinct, hard-corrected sub-rules (permanent placement over /tmp, mandatory dry-run and view-only flags, npm-isolated subdirectories for Node tools) from a single session with explicit CORRECTION tags. The pattern is stable, specific, and will recur on any developer-tooling task. It has no corresponding rules file yet — it exists only in memory, which is session-gated.

feedback_cache_blast_radius.md, feedback_client_env_exposure.md, and feedback_pushback_honesty.md all encode the same meta-behavior across different projects and session IDs: proactively surface risks (cache scope, secret exposure, factual errors) without waiting for user correction. Three-project recurrence on a single behavioral theme is the canonical graduation signal. Encoding this as a rules file makes it available to agents that don't load the memory corpus.

feedback_data_integrity.md and feedback_pipeline_ux.md are both 19 days old and tied to a single data-transformation session (originSessionId 025d513b). Their rules — no hallucinated values, no dropped columns, versioned output with spot-checks, wizard UX for ambiguous cells — are correct but domain-specific to CSV/Excel pipeline work. The user's active workloads (Next.js frontend, Python microservices, Claude Code tooling) do not produce data-pipeline contexts where these rules fire. Loading them at session start adds noise without benefit.
